rules.title: Prefer clean-break repository and workflow boundaries
rules.directive: For repo-level architecture work, default toward feature-owned repositories and workflows, an HTTP-ready contract, and clean-break boundaries instead of preserving old shared app-state hooks for compatibility.
antiPatterns: Preserving shared snapshot contracts only for backwards compatibility
Mixing feature workflow logic back into shell providers
rules.overlayReason: Upstream React Native skills do not encode this repo's chosen architecture direction or clean-break preference.

rules.title: Keep shell providers thin and out of feature cycles
rules.directive: Keep shell providers such as AppProvider shell-only. When shell code needs feature state, query repositories directly instead of importing feature query hooks and recreating provider-to-feature cycles.
antiPatterns: Provider imports from feature query hooks
Shared shell state coupled to feature-layer hooks
rules.overlayReason: This is a repo-specific architectural constraint derived from prior regressions.

rules.title: Push toward reusable hooks, components, and smaller files
rules.directive: When refactoring screens, split them into a thin shell plus hooks and reusable components with explicit file-size pressure rather than leaving large screen-local monoliths.
antiPatterns: Huge screen files that mix data, layout, and local helpers
Single extracted helper files that keep growing after the first split
rules.overlayReason: The repo has explicit line-cap and extraction preferences that are narrower than generic React Native design guidance.

rules.title: Check worktree state before broad structural changes
rules.directive: Before broad refactors or repo sweeps, inspect git status and isolate the work so existing in-progress feature edits are not clobbered.
antiPatterns: Sweeping refactors in a dirty worktree without checking current edits
rules.overlayReason: This is a workflow rule driven by the user's actual multi-stream working style in this repo.

rules.title: Start from the exact file, route, or stack trace
triggerPhrases: why am i getting this error
rules.directive: When the user pastes an exact stack trace, route error, or file path, inspect that precise on-disk module and code path first instead of jumping to generic config or environment theories.
antiPatterns: Starting with alias or config debugging before checking whether the imported file exists
General troubleshooting lists that do not address the first hard failure in the pasted log
rules.overlayReason: This is a repo-specific debugging workflow preference, not generic mobile guidance.

rules.title: Separate machine-state failures from repo-state failures
rules.directive: When a failure is caused by local toolchain or machine setup, identify and state that immediately instead of editing application code first.
antiPatterns: Changing app code to chase missing Fastlane, missing simulator runtimes, or PATH problems
rules.overlayReason: This is a concrete failure-handling pattern from repeated local iOS build issues in this repo.

rules.title: Prefer the real native iOS path over lookalikes
rules.directive: When the user asks for actual native iOS behavior or appearance, use the true native platform path that already exists in the repo or dependency stack instead of building a JavaScript approximation.
antiPatterns: Custom blur or floating bar approximations when Expo Router native tabs already exist
Fake iOS controls when a native control path is available
rules.overlayReason: Upstream skills cover native feel broadly, but not this repo's explicit 'restore the real native path first' standard.

rules.title: Make profile and settings surfaces feel Apple-native
rules.directive: For profile and settings surfaces, default toward grouped settings panes, quieter surfaces, compact rows, and real detail screens with working controls rather than generic card stacks or placeholder labels.
antiPatterns: Generic card piles for settings-style screens
Rows that navigate nowhere or only show placeholder text
rules.overlayReason: The repo needs a stronger product-specific default for profile/settings surfaces than the upstream HIG summary alone provides.

rules.title: Prefer native picker patterns over custom stepper controls
rules.directive: For time selection and lightweight upload choices, prefer familiar row-triggered native picker and platform dialog patterns over custom steppers, oversized sheets, or invented controls.
antiPatterns: Custom stepper-based time input flows
Route-backed upload UIs for a simple camera-or-files choice
rules.overlayReason: The local product rule is stricter than general Android guidance: keep these flows lightweight and native instead of route-heavy.

rules.title: Prefer action-first compact rows over low-value status copy
rules.directive: When a row already communicates the required action, prefer compact action-first rows and keep status labels only when they materially improve comprehension.
antiPatterns: Redundant status text that restates the obvious action
Extra helper copy that increases row height without adding meaning
rules.overlayReason: This is a user-specific product judgment rule, not something upstream design packs can infer.

rules.title: Prune low-signal data and exact visible clutter aggressively
rules.directive: When the user calls out low-value copy or stats, delete the exact visible block and re-center the screen around its primary job instead of softening or renaming the clutter.
antiPatterns: Decorative dashboards that mix multiple goals on one employee screen
Empty footer shells or card shells left behind after removing copy
rules.overlayReason: This is a strong user-specific product-editing preference derived from repeated screenshot feedback.

rules.title: Prefer contextual local UI fixes after the shared primitive is proven wrong in-context
rules.directive: Once the problem is clearly local to a card or screen context, prefer a contextual local styling or component treatment instead of continuing to churn the shared primitive.
antiPatterns: Repeatedly stretching a shared button to fit a compact card use case
Broad shared-component churn for a clearly local visibility problem
rules.overlayReason: This rule is highly contextual and comes directly from repeated user correction about in-card actions.

rules.title: Treat blue-accent buttons with white text as a design-system rule
triggerPhrases: white text and never black text
rules.directive: When accent-filled controls are blue, treat white foreground text as a shared design-system rule and fix it at the token or shared component layer instead of one screen at a time.
antiPatterns: One-off foreground overrides on individual screens
Accent-filled controls that render dark text
rules.overlayReason: This is a concrete repo-local visual rule set by the user.
